1.description: Hourly and geographic crime distribution Panel A illustrates the hourly distribution of crimes in the NIBRS database from January 1991 to December 2015. The bars depict the percentage of daily crimes that occur within the specified hour. All crime incidents listed in Table 1 are represented by the solid bars, while only the violent crime incidents of assault and homicide are represented by the dashed bars. Panel B illustrates all city police and county sheriff agencies in the NIBRS database. Agencies included in the analysis are displayed as circles, while those excluded over the entire analysis are displayed as x’s. Lines represent region borders as defined in Nelson and Rae (2016).

2.description: NIBRS coverage These figures illustrate the average daily coverage of the NIBRS database from January 1991 to December 2015, averaged over each month, after the filters described in the text. Agencies describe the number of law enforcement agencies. Population only includes the population that the agencies cover and is measured in hundreds of thousands. Crimes is the aggregate number of crime incidents for all offenses listed in Table 1, while violent crimes is the number of assaults and homicides. Average crime rates are per 100 million people and calculated as the number of incidents in each agency divided by the agency population.

3.description: Market returns and violent crime rates randomization test These figures illustrate the distribution of coefficients for the market return from 10,000 randomization test iterations. Each randomization test regresses violent crime rates on market returns that are assigned to random trading dates without replacement. The specification used is the same as in panel B of Table 3. The solid line represents the histogram of the market return coefficient from 10,000 iterations, while the dashed line indicates the value of the market return coefficient from panel B of Table 3. Panel A shows the distribution for high-income locations, while panel B shows the distribution for low-income locations. Additional statistics on the distribution can be found in Table IA.13 in the Internet Appendix.

4.description: Stockholder and nonstockholder crime propensity This figure illustrates the hypothetical crime propensity (marginal utility) for stockholders and nonstockholders for a given market return.
